title: Preparation of cell-sized water-in-oil droplets for in vitro reconstitution of biological processes in cellular compartments
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"j6emx","text":"This protocol presents a method for encapsulation of purified proteins into cell-sized water-in-oil droplets surrounded by a phospholipid monolayer, which can be broadly applied to studies to reconstitute biological processes in cellular compartments.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"b0ldr","text":"In the past decade, in vitro reconstitution of biological processes from component molecules has been regarded as a powerful approach to uncover the underlying design principles of cells1. This bottom-up approach enables independent, precise control of biochemical parameters, such as protein composition and concentration, and physical parameters, such as boundary conditions and system size. Thus, the minimal necessary components and specific physical conditions essential for realizing biological functions can be identified.\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":20,"length":8},{"style":"sup","offset":186,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"x6pnj","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1wxcv","text":"\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"wxo27","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"j91hq","text":"Here, we present a simple protocol for encapsulating purified proteins into cell-sized water-in-oil droplets surrounded by a phospholipid monolayer to mimic the cell boundary. This water-in-oil droplet system has been extensively utilized to investigate the effects of cellular microenvironments on various biological processes, including the self-organization of cytoskeletal architecture2–8 and gene expression9,10. We adopted this system to elucidate the direct contribution of the cellular boundary to the assembly of the cytokinetic ring, and clarified that actin filaments and myosin motors can self-organize into ring-shaped contractile bundles when they are confined to a cell-sized spherical space, even without spatiotemporal regulatory signals11. The protocol described below was according to our previous reports11,12.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":389,"length":5},{"style":"sup","offset":412,"length":4},{"style":"sup","offset":754,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":824,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Dissolve phospholipids in chloroform (dehydrated with molecular sieves) to a final concentration of 20 mM.
Dispense the lipid solution into glass test tubes (50 μL per tube).
Place the test tubes in a vacuum desiccator.
Evacuate overnight at room temperature in the dark to completely remove chloroform (Fig. 1a).
Store lipid dry films in a vacuum desiccator at room temperature in the dark. The lipid dry films should be used within 1 month.
Tips: Phospholipids should be stored at −20 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere in the dark.
Mix mineral oil by gentle inversion just before use.
Collect a lipid dry film sample from a vacuum desiccator.
Place 1 mL of mineral oil into the glass test tube.
Seal the top of the tube with a heat-resistant sealing film (Fig. 1b).
Incubate the tube at 80 °C for ~15 min in a dry bath incubator.
Dissolve the lipid dry film by vortexing (~10 s; Fig. 1c).
If the lipid dry film remains at the bottom of the tube, repeat steps 10 and 11.
Set the tube in a floating rack and float in a water bath sonicator.
Sonicate the tube for 90 min at 60 °C and 60 W power (Fig. 1d).
Immediately after sonication, mix the lipid-oil mixture by vortexing (~10 s).
Cool to room temperature and incubate overnight at room temperature in the dark.
The concentration of lipids in oil is 1 mM. Store the lipid-oil mixture at room temperature in the dark, and use within 1 week of preparation.
Place two pieces of double-sided adhesive tape (thickness: 100 μm) onto a silicone-coated coverslip with a 8 mm gap between them.
Place a normal coverslip on top of the gap, to produce a chamber of 18 mm × 8 mm × 100 μm (see Fig. 2c).
Mix lipid-oil mixture by vortexing immediately before use.
Transfer 80 μL of the lipid-oil mixture into a 1.5-mL microcentrifuge tube pre-equilibrated to a desired temperature.
Pipet 2 μL of protein solution directly into the lipid-oil mixture in the microcentrifuge tube (Fig. 2a).
Mix the lipid-oil mixture and protein solutions by vortexing for 10–30 s to form water-in-oil droplets (Fig. 2b). The droplet size can be controlled by optimizing the power and duration of vortexing.
Perfuse the water-in-oil emulsion into a flow chamber (Fig. 2c).
Seal the flow chamber with Valap (Fig. 2d).
Observe the water-in-oil droplets using an optical microscope (Fig. 3).

• Preparation of lipid dry films requires an overnight step. The lipid dry films should be stored in a vacuum desiccator at room temperature in the dark, and used within 1 month of preparation. • Preparation of lipid-oil mixture is performed in 1 day. The lipid-oil mixture should be stored at room temperature in the dark, and used within 1 week of preparation. • Encapsulation of proteins in water-in-oil droplets takes ~5 min.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

Cell-sized spherical confinement induces the spontaneous formation of contractile actomyosin rings in vitro by Makito Miyazaki, Masataka Chiba, Hiroki Eguchi, Takashi Ohki, and Shin’ichi Ishiwata published in Nature Cell Biology on 24 Mar, 2015 https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/ncb3142 Quantitative Analysis of the Lamellarity of Giant Liposomes Prepared by the Inverted Emulsion Method by Masataka Chiba, Makito Miyazaki, and Shin’ichi Ishiwata published in Biophysical Journal on 20 Mar, 2015 https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.bpj.2014.05.039

Figure 1. Preparation of lipid-oil mixture. <img src="http://www.nature.com/protocolexchange/system/uploads/3571/original/fig1 v1 00.tif?1426826338"> To demonstrate the process of dissolving a lipid dry film, egg PC containing 0.5% (mol/mol) of rhodamine-labelled phospholipids (1,2-dioleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-lissamine rhodamine B sulfonyl ammonium salt; 810150C, Avanti Polar Lipids, Alabama, USA) was used. (*a*) Formation of a lipid dry film at the bottom of a glass test tube by overnight evacuation. (*b*) Overlaying a lipid dry film with mineral oil and sealing the tube top with a heat-resistant film. (*c*) Dissolving the lipid dry film by heating and vortexing. (*d*) Sonication in a water bath sonicator. Scale bars, 1 cm. Figure 2. Encapsulation of proteins into cell-sized water-in-oil droplets. <img src="http://www.nature.com/protocolexchange/system/uploads/3573/original/fig2 v1 01.tif?1426826871"> Tetramethylrhodamine-labelled bovine serum albumin (TMR-BSA, 5 mg mL^-1^) in PBS was encapsulated. Egg PC was used for phospholipids. (*a*) Before vortexing. The yellow arrow indicates the TMR-BSA solution in the lipid-oil mixture. (*b*) After vortexing. Many cell-sized water-in-oil droplets were formed. (*c*) Perfusion of water-in-oil droplets into a flow chamber. (*d*) Sealing the four sides of the flow chamber with Valap to prevent flow and evaporation of water from the droplets. Scale bars, 1 cm. Figure 3. Observation of water-in-oil droplets under an optical microscope. <img src="http://www.nature.com/protocolexchange/system/uploads/3577/original/fig3 v1 01.tif?1426831968"> A bright-field image, a fluorescence image, and the merged image of droplets containing 5 mg mL^-1^ TMR-BSA in PBS. An epifluorescence inverted microscope (custom-built) equipped with a ×60 objective (PlanApo ×60 NA 1.45 Oil TIRFM, Olympus) and an electron-multiplying CCD (charge-coupled device) camera (iXon 3, Andor Technology) was used. Phospholipid, egg PC; scale bar, 20 μm.
